name: Boise warehouse ignored OSHA rules on fumes—what evidence do I need from old coworkers?

mainEntity.name: Boise warehouse ignored OSHA rules on fumes—what evidence do I need from old coworkers?

acceptedAnswer.text: You need statements confirming that ventilation was broken or PPE was not provided. Obtaining OSHA violation records and safety logs is also essential to prove the company knew the environment was hazardous but failed to act.
acceptedAnswer.description:
If the refinery or a manufacturer ignored OSHA regulations regarding fume extraction or respirators, it significantly strengthens your claim for benefits. While workers\' comp usually doesn't care about "fault," proof of OSHA violations can be powerful evidence during an appeal if the insurance company tries to claim your work environment was safe. In some states, gross negligence can even open the door for additional penalties against the employer.

Key evidence from OSHA:
- Copies of any "Notice of Violation" or fines paid by the company.
- Testimonies from former coworkers about broken ventilation systems.
- Evidence that you complained about the fumes but were ignored.

Request a copy of the OSHA inspection report through a Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) request. Even if OSHA hasn't visited yet, you can file a complaint now to trigger an investigation. A lawyer can help navigate these federal records to build a "negligence-plus" case that ensures the insurance company takes your claim seriously.
